This was a nice adaptation, though I still prefer the 1994 one.
I read the book in Spanish when I was 15 years old. ❤️
I'm seeing a lot of comments requoting that one scene from Jo, but leaving the last part out.
Why?
Why is modern "feminism" afraid of love? Afraid to admit that men and women NEED each other for many things in life?

A woman can be successful and empowered at the same time she is loving and being loved.
We can't fight fire with fire, as that will only bring total chaos.
As a woman who supports equality, I can't stand behind today's shenanigans... I just can't.
Putting down others WON'T empower us, ladies.
Turning our backs on normal and natural things of life such as falling in love and yearning for life long happiness with someone else, won't make us weaker.

We can be successful, empowered AND have love in our hearts. ❤️
This is something the 1994 adaptation did better in portraying.

text: If you read Louisa's letter exchange with her publisher she was the one who came up with all the marriages. Friedrichs character in little women is based on philosopher Henry Thoreau Louisa was in love with him. Laurie is based on Ladislas Wisniewski a young man who was first flirting with Louisa and then betrayed him. In all of Louisa's novels these two male archetypes are constantly present. The Friedrich archetype is the humble hard working man (Fritz in lw, Mac in Rose in Bloom, David in Work) He has silent passion for the protagonist and the Louisa type of protagonist loves him and wants that equal relationship.  Laurie archetype is charming but idle. He either puts his act together and falls for a female character (like Amy) who is stern with them  or they fail and fet sucked into consuming toxic self-harming behavior. Little Women is all about Louisa's love life. That is why she had conflicted relationship towards it. Henry was 16 years older (same age difference than between Louisa and the professor) Ladislas was 10 years younger. Louisa and Henry, They did have an affair and almost a telepathic way of communication which is also present in the novels. Henry passed away 5years before LW was published.  That is also why Louisa listed out to be nurse in the war. He had lost her sister Lizzie at the same time.

text: I think Amy was honestly the most strongest character in this movie, she was selfless, smart, she knew exactly what she needed and she realistically knew how she was going to survive in this era of time. Jo was the main character but she never really had any sort of character development, she was always selfish and only ever thought of what she wanted NOT what she needed. Amy knew that the one way in which she was going to truly be able to live her life was to marry rich and that was literally the only way a women could be wealthy, Amy was just as ambitious as Jo and she also had dreams of her own but she saw that her eldest sister, Meg, married a poor man out of love and that man wasn't really able to support anyone but himself and her, Jo wasn't even going to get married, which, although was very courageous, it was also insanely stupid, at a time like that where women literally don't have a name for themselves there was no way that Jo would be able to earn enough to support her entire family. Amy realises this and she knew what she had to do, she knew that she was the only one out of her sisters that still had a chance of marrying rich therefore she selflessly decided she was going to marry fred out of wealth not love. Throughout the whole story everyone's only focused on Jo's dreams and they don't even realise Amy's ambition to be an artist, even when aunt march decides that she wants to take Amy to paris not Jo, everyone automatically feels bad for Jo even tho we all know that Jo wouldn't be happy in a place where she's expected to act all ladylike and artistic, for Amy this was literally what she's been waiting for her entire life, a place where she could truly take her passion for art forward. Amy has always loved Laurie, Jo never did, yet she was still the girl that Laurie first fell in love with. No one even thought about how that could make Amy feel, she knows that even if Laurie ever chooses her later on, she would be the second choice. When Laurie finally realised that his love for Jo was only platonic, he grew to love Amy and when he realised how much he loved Amy, they got married so why did everyone decide to automatically blame Amy instead of Jo for not thinking smarter. everyone hated Amy for being so girly, acting like a normal women and wanting to get married, she was seen as the husband stealer when really Jo always had first choice in who could marry Laurie. people loved Jo because she was different and she "wasn't like other girls", they didn't even think about how selfish and ignorant she could get.

Watched this film yesterday. One of the best movie adaptations created this year. Got me all over my feels!
THE STRUGGLE OF A FEMINIST

Six remakes since the original nearly a hundred years ago, and this one is different....how? 
Jo: Free-spirit, a writer, hot-tempered, non-conformist, longs for love
Meg: Lovely, demure, studious, dutiful, honorable
Beth: Sweet, peace-maker, quiet, beloved, everyone's heart
Amy: Spoiled, jealous, headstrong, reckless, an artist, follows her heart
Marmee: Feminist, unofficial head of household, protector of her girls
Laurie: Smitten with Jo, confidant with Meg, mourns the loss of Beth, marries Amy

Jo writes stories. Arranges plays for her and her sisters to act out. Is close to her Aunt March. Cuts off her heair and sells it to buy a ticket home for their father who is off fighting. Pursued by Laurie. Meg is happy to be a housewife (much to Jo's chagrin). Beth contracts scarlet fever. Amy causes trouble. Burns Jo's manuscript. Fight ensues. Amy nearly dies. Everyone kisses and makes up. Meg gets married. Jo rebuffs Laurie; sells her manuscript. Becomes a teacher. Amy goes to Paris and is praised for her paintings. Marries Laurie. Jo gets married.

Tell me how anything has changed in 80 years--other than the actresses? Yeah, I think I'll pass.

This movie is all-round bad. Its poorly written script fundamentally changes the original story just to inject a ‘faux’ feminist overtone. So is its casting. In trying to distinguish it from previous movie adaptations (and presupposing its audience already knows the story), this movie randomly flips the timeline and sequence of events back-and-forth. Not only does it kill plot cohesion, it fails to give most actors a chance to develop their roles. Purporting to address the issue of feminism of this era (Massachusetts during the Civil War in 1860’s), it blasts marriage as merely an ‘economic proposition’, which is true - except it does so casually in 3 lines of one dialog. 

In my view, the 1949 (Elizabeth Taylor) and 1994 (Susan Sarandon) adaptations of ‘Little Women’ are fantastic classics. Why they bother to make such an inferior one in 2019 really beats me. Only guess is : director Greta Gerwig has no clothes !
